commentaries.author: Augustine of Hippo
commentaries.content: The law leads to knowledge of sin and at length to the transgression of the law itself. It is thus with the knowledge and increase of sin that grace may be sought through faith.

commentaries.author: Cornelius a Lapide
commentaries.content: But that no man is justified. This is a fourth proof. S. Paul would fain convince the Galatians by an accumulation of proofs. After that based on the example of Abraham, and that on the condition of those under the law, he proceeds to another drawn from Habukkuk ii4 , a text already explained in the notes on Romans 1:17.

commentaries.author: Ignatius of Antioch
commentaries.content: And be ye steadfast, "for the just shall live by faith; "

commentaries.author: John Chrysostom
commentaries.content: All have sinned and are under a curse. Yet he does not say this, lest he should seem to be running ahead of his own demonstration. He establishes it by a testimony that succinctly proves both that no one fulfills the law (and therefore they are accursed) and that faith justifies…. For the prophet did not say, “The just shall live from law” but from faith.

commentaries.author: John Chrysostom
commentaries.content: For all have sinned, and are under the curse. However he does not say this yet, lest he should seem to lay it down of himself, but here again establishes his point by a text which concisely states both points; that no man has fulfilled the Law, (wherefore they are under the curse,) and, that Faith justifies. What then is the text? It is in the book of the prophet Habakkuk, The just shall live by faith, Habakkuk 2:4 which not only establishes the righteousness that is of Faith, but also that there is no salvation through the Law. As no one, he says, kept the Law, but all were under the curse, on account of transgression, an easy way was provided, that from Faith, which is in itself a strong proof that no man can be justified by the Law. For the prophet says not, The just shall live by the Law, but, by faith:

commentaries.author: Tertullian of Carthage
commentaries.content: But where three are, a church is, albeit they be laics. For each individual lives by his own faith,
